Decision Matrix — When to Use What ### Rendering Strategy | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | Static content, rarely changes | SSG (`generateStaticParams`) | Fastest, cached at edge | | Static with periodic updates | ISR (`revalidate`) | Fresh enough, still fast | | Per-request dynamic data | SSR (Server Components) | Always fresh, streamed | | Mix of static shell + dynamic parts | Cache Components (`'use cache'`) | Best of both worlds | | Real-time interactive UI | Client Components | Full browser API access | ### Data Mutations | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | Form submissions, in-app mutations | Server Actions | Integrated with caching, progressive enhancement | | Public API, webhooks, large uploads | Route Handlers | REST semantics, streaming support | | Scheduled tasks | Cron Jobs + Serverless Functions | Reliable scheduling | ### AI Features | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | **Any AI feature (default)** | **AI Gateway** (`model: 'provider/model'`) | **Failover, cost tracking, observability — no provider API keys needed on Vercel** | | **Any streaming AI UI (default)** | **AI Elements** (`npx ai-elements`) + AI SDK `useChat` | **Handles UIMessage parts, streaming markdown, tool calls, reasoning — no manual rendering** | | **Any AI-generated text (mandatory)** | **AI Elements ``** | **Universal markdown renderer — never render AI text as raw `{text}`. Use for chat, workflows, reports, notifications** | | Chat interface | AI SDK `useChat` + `streamText` + AI Gateway + AI Elements | Streaming UI, provider-agnostic | | Chat UI components (messages, tools, reasoning) | AI Elements (`npx ai-elements`) | Pre-built, handles UIMessage parts | | Custom chat rendering (no AI Elements) | Manual `message.parts` iteration | Full control, see ⤳ skill: json-render | | Image generation (default) | AI Gateway `model: 'google/gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview'` + `generateText` → `result.files` | Multimodal LLM, best quality, gateway-native | | Image generation (image-only models) | `experimental_generateImage` (Imagen 4.0, Flux 2) | Only for dedicated image models, not multimodal LLMs | | Structured data extraction | AI SDK `generateText` + `Output.object()` + AI Gateway | Type-safe, schema-validated | | Multi-step agent | AI SDK `Agent` class + AI Gateway | Loop control, tool calling | | Production agent (must not lose state) | Workflow DevKit `DurableAgent` | Survives crashes, observable | | Provider-specific features (e.g., computer use) | Direct provider SDK (`@ai-sdk/anthropic`) | Only when gateway doesn't expose the feature | | Connect to external tools | AI SDK MCP Client | Standard protocol, OAuth | | Agent needs live Vercel state | Vercel MCP Server | Read projects, deployments, logs via MCP | | Multi-platform chat bot (Slack, Teams, Discord, Telegram, etc.) | Chat SDK (`chat` + `@chat-adapter/*`) | Single codebase, unified API, cards, streaming | | Chat bot with AI responses | Chat SDK + AI SDK (`thread.post(textStream)`) | Streaming AI across all platforms | | UI generation from prompts | v0 | Visual output, GitHub integration | **IMPORTANT**: Default to AI Gateway for all AI features. Only use direct provider SDKs (`@ai-sdk/anthropic`, `@ai-sdk/openai`, etc.) when you need provider-specific features not exposed through the gateway. ### Storage | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | File uploads, media | Vercel Blob | First-party, up to 5TB | | Feature flags, A/B config | Edge Config | Ultra-low latency at edge | | Relational database | Neon (via Marketplace) | Serverless Postgres, branching | | Key-value cache | Upstash Redis (via Marketplace) | Serverless Redis, same billing | ### Build & Monorepo | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | Single Next.js app | Turbopack (default) | Fastest HMR, built-in | | Monorepo with multiple apps/packages | Turborepo | Caching, parallelism, affected | | Code quality enforcement in monorepo | Conformance | Automated best-practice checks | | Non-Next.js framework | Framework-native bundler | Vercel adapters handle deploy | ### Security | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | DDoS protection | Vercel Firewall (automatic) | Always on, all plans | | Custom traffic rules | WAF rules engine | Framework-aware, 300ms propagation | | Bot blocking | Bot Filter | One-click, public beta | | Rate limiting | WAF rate limiting | Per-endpoint control | | OWASP protection | Managed rulesets (Enterprise) | Industry-standard rules | | Compliance isolation (SOC2, HIPAA) | Secure Compute | Dedicated infrastructure, no shared tenancy | | Tokenless CI/CD deployments | OIDC Federation | Short-lived tokens, no secrets to rotate | ### Functions | Need | Use | Why | |------|-----|-----| | Standard server logic | Serverless Functions (Node.js) | Full Node.js, up to 14min (paid) | | Ultra-low latency, simple logic | Edge Functions | <1ms cold start, global | | Long-running with I/O waits | Fluid Compute | Shared instances, waitUntil | | AI streaming responses | Streaming Functions | SSE, zero config | | Scheduled execution | Cron Jobs | vercel.json schedule config | ### Disambiguation: Interception Compute These three mechanisms all intercept or handle requests before your application logic runs. Choose based on **where** the interception happens and **what** you need to do. | Mechanism | Layer | Runtime | Use When | Avoid When | |-----------|-------|---------|----------|------------| | **Routing Middleware** (`middleware.ts` / platform-level) | Edge Network, before cache | V8 isolates (Web Standard APIs) | Auth checks, geo-redirects, A/B routing, header rewriting — any framework | You need Node.js APIs, heavy computation, or database access | | **`proxy.ts`** (Next.js 16+) | Application layer, replaces `middleware.ts` | Node.js | Same use cases as Routing Middleware but you need `node:*` modules, ORM calls, or full Node.js compat | You're not on Next.js 16+; prefer Routing Middleware for non-Next.js frameworks | | **Edge Functions** | Edge Network, handles the full request | V8 isolates (Web Standard APIs) | Ultra-low-latency API endpoints, simple compute at the edge, streaming responses | You need Node.js runtime, long execution times, or large dependencies | > **Key distinction**: Routing Middleware and `proxy.ts` are *interceptors* — they rewrite, redirect, or annotate requests before the handler runs. Edge Functions *are* the handler — they produce the response. If you previously used Next.js `middleware.ts` and are upgrading to Next.js 16, rename to `proxy.ts` (see § Migration Awareness). ⤳ skill: routing-middleware — Platform-level request interception ⤳ skill: vercel-functions — Edge Functions and Serverless Functions ⤳ skill: nextjs — `proxy.ts` in Next.js 16 ### Disambiguation: Caching Layers Three distinct caching systems serve different purposes. They can be used independently or layered together. | Mechanism | Scope | Invalidation | Use When | Avoid When | |-----------|-------|-------------|----------|------------| | **Next.js Cache** (`'use cache'`, `revalidate`, `revalidatePath/Tag`) | Per-route or per-component, framework-managed | Time-based (`revalidate: N`), on-demand (`revalidateTag()`, `revalidatePath()`) | Caching rendered pages, component trees, or data fetches within a Next.js app | You need caching outside Next.js, or need to cache arbitrary key-value data | | **Runtime Cache** (Vercel platform, per-region KV) | Per-region key-value store, any framework | Tag-based (`purgeByTag()`), key-based (`delete()`) | Caching expensive computations, API responses, or shared data across functions — works with any framework on Vercel | You only need page-level caching (use Next.js Cache instead); you need global consistency (Runtime Cache is per-region) | | **CDN Cache + Purge-by-Tag** (Edge Network, `Cache-Control` + `Cache-Tag` headers) | Global CDN edge, HTTP-level | `Cache-Control` TTL, on-demand purge via Vercel API (`POST /v1/edge-config/purge`) | Static assets, ISR pages, any HTTP response you want cached globally at the edge | Dynamic per-user content, responses that must never be stale | > **Layering pattern**: A typical Next.js app uses all three — Next.js Cache for component/route-level freshness, Runtime Cache for shared cross-request data (e.g., product catalog), and CDN Cache for static assets and ISR pages. Each layer has its own invalidation strategy; tag-based invalidation can cascade across layers when configured. ⤳ skill: runtime-cache — Per-region key-value caching with tag-based invalidation ⤳ skill: nextjs — `'use cache'`, `revalidatePath`, `revalidateTag` --- ## 11. Common Cross-Product Workflows ### 1. Build an AI Chatbot ``` 1. vercel link (or create project in dashboard) 2. Enable AI Gateway in Vercel dashboard → auto-provisions OIDC credentials 3. vercel env pull (pulls VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN + gateway env vars to .env.local) 4. npm install ai @ai-sdk/react (core SDK + React hooks — `@ai-sdk/react` is required for `useChat`) 5. npx ai-elements (install chat UI components — Message, Conversation, PromptInput) 6. Code: model: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6' (plain string routes through AI Gateway automatically) 7. Server: convertToModelMessages(messages) → streamText → toUIMessageStreamResponse() 8. Client: useChat({ transport: new DefaultChatTransport({ api: '/api/chat' }) }) 9. Next.js (App Router) → AI SDK + AI Elements → AI Gateway (OIDC auth) → Vercel Functions (streaming) → vercel deploy ``` **OIDC Authentication (default):** When you run `vercel env pull`, it provisions a `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` — a short-lived JWT that the AI Gateway uses automatically. No manual API keys needed. The `@ai-sdk/gateway` package reads `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` from the environment via `@vercel/oidc`. On Vercel deployments, OIDC tokens are auto-refreshed. For local dev, re-run `vercel env pull` if the token expires (~24h). ``` ### 2. Build a Multi-Platform Chat Bot ``` 1. npm install chat @chat-adapter/slack @chat-adapter/telegram @chat-adapter/state-redis 2. Create lib/bot.ts → new Chat({ adapters: { slack, telegram }, state: createRedisState() }) 3. Register handlers: onNewMention, onSubscribedMessage, onAction 4. Create webhook routes (for example app/api/bot/slack/route.ts and app/api/bot/telegram/route.ts) → bot.webhooks.(req, { waitUntil }) 5. For AI responses: npm install ai → thread.post(result.textStream) 6. For rich messages: use Card JSX → renders to each platform's native card format 7. Deploy to Vercel → configure SLACK_BOT_TOKEN, SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, REDIS_URL 8. Add more platforms: npm install @chat-adapter/discord @chat-adapter/teams @chat-adapter/telegram → add to adapters map → one webhook route per platform ``` ### 3. Build a Durable AI Agent ``` 1. vercel link → enable AI Gateway → vercel env pull (OIDC credentials required) 2. Next.js (API Route) → Workflow DevKit (DurableAgent) → AI SDK (tool calling) → AI Gateway (OIDC auth for model strings in workflow steps) → Neon Postgres (state) → Vercel Functions (step execution) 3. For human-in-the-loop: defineHook() + getWritable() token emission + resumeHook() route 4. For AI text in workflow events: use from AI Elements (not raw text) ``` ### 4. Full-Stack SaaS App ``` Next.js (App Router) → Neon Postgres (data) → Clerk (auth, via Marketplace) → Stripe (payments, via Marketplace) → Vercel Blob (uploads) → Edge Config (feature flags) → Vercel Analytics ``` **Starter kit**: Use `npx next-forge@latest init` to scaffold a production-ready SaaS monorepo with all of the above pre-wired (plus email, observability, security, AI, i18n, and more). ⤳ skill: next-forge **Clerk integration gotchas**: - `vercel integration add clerk` requires terms acceptance in the terminal (AI agents are blocked — user must run it manually) - After CLI install, the user must complete setup in the Vercel Dashboard to connect Clerk to the project - Clerk auto-provisions `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`, but you must manually set `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_IN_URL=/sign-in` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_UP_URL=/sign-up` - **Organization flow**: After sign-in, if the user has no organization, `auth()` returns `{ userId, orgSlug: null }`. Handle this explicitly — redirect to an org creation page or show ``. Without this, the app will loop back to the landing page endlessly. - The `proxy.ts` (or `middleware.ts`) must call `clerkMiddleware()` for `auth()` to work in Server Components. If proxy is in the wrong location, you get: `Clerk: auth() was called without Clerk middleware` ### 5. Monorepo with Multiple Apps ``` Turborepo (orchestration) → Next.js App A → Vercel Platform (deploy) → Next.js App B → Vercel Platform (deploy) → Shared packages → Turbopack (bundling) → Remote Cache → Vercel (shared across CI) ``` ### 6. Deploy with Custom CI ``` Git Push → CI Pipeline → vercel build → vercel deploy --prebuilt → VERCEL_TOKEN auth → Preview URL → vercel promote (production) ``` --- ## 12. --- ## Conventions ### UI Design Defaults - For application UI, default to **shadcn/ui + Geist**. Do not build core controls from raw HTML plus ad-hoc Tailwind when design-system primitives exist. - Default to **dark mode** for dashboards, AI products, internal tools, and developer surfaces. Use light mode when the product is clearly content-first or editorial. - Favor **zinc/neutral/slate tokens**, one accent color, and clear borders over scattered rainbow accents, heavy gradients, and random glassmorphism. - Let **type, spacing, and composition** create hierarchy: Tabs + Card + Form for settings, Card + Table + Filters for dashboards, Sheet for mobile navigation, AlertDialog for destructive confirmation. - Use **Geist Sans** for interface text and **Geist Mono** for code, metrics, IDs, timestamps, and commands. - Avoid generic UI output: raw buttons, clickable divs, repeated bordered card grids, inconsistent radii, and forgotten empty/loading/error states. ### Next.js 16 - Default to Server Components. Only add `'use client'` when you need interactivity or browser APIs. - Push `'use client'` boundaries as far down the component tree as possible. - Use Server Actions (`'use server'`) for data mutations, not Route Handlers (unless building a public API). - All request APIs are async in Next.js 16: `await cookies()`, `await headers()`, `await params`, `await searchParams`. - Use `proxy.ts` instead of `middleware.ts` (Next.js 16 rename). Proxy runs on Node.js runtime only. **Location**: place `proxy.ts` at the same level as `app/` — at project root normally, or inside `src/` if using `--src-dir`. - Turbopack config is top-level in `next.config.ts`, not under `experimental.turbopack`. - Use Cache Components (`'use cache'`) instead of PPR for mixing static and dynamic content. - Prefer `next/image` for images and `next/font` for fonts — both optimize automatically on Vercel. - `@vercel/postgres` and `@vercel/kv` are sunset — use `@neondatabase/serverless` and `@upstash/redis`. ### AI SDK v6 - **Default to AI Gateway** — pass `"provider/model"` strings directly (e.g., `model: 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6'`) — they route through the AI Gateway automatically. The `gateway()` wrapper from `'ai'` is optional and only needed when using `providerOptions.gateway` for routing/failover/tags. Do NOT install or import direct provider SDKs (`@ai-sdk/anthropic`, `@ai-sdk/openai`, etc.) unless you need provider-specific features not exposed through the gateway. - **Install `@ai-sdk/react` for React hooks** — `useChat`, `useCompletion`, and `useObject` live in `@ai-sdk/react` (not `ai`). Always `npm install ai @ai-sdk/react` together for React/Next.js projects. - **OIDC is the default auth for AI Gateway** — when you run `vercel env pull`, it provisions `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` which the `@ai-sdk/gateway` package reads automatically via `@vercel/oidc`. No `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` or provider-specific API keys needed. On Vercel deployments, OIDC tokens are auto-refreshed. For local dev, re-run `vercel env pull` if the token expires (~24h). - **For AI projects, set up a Vercel project first** — run `vercel link` (or create via dashboard) → enable AI Gateway in dashboard → `vercel env pull` to get OIDC credentials locally. Do NOT manually create `.env.local` with provider-specific API keys like `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or `OPENAI_API_KEY`. - **AI Elements is MANDATORY for all AI-generated text** — `npx ai-elements@latest` must be installed immediately after scaffolding. Never render AI text as raw `{text}` or `

{content}

` — it shows ugly `**`, `##`, `---`. Use `` for chat with `useChat`, and `` (from `@/components/ai-elements/message`) for any other AI markdown (workflow events, reports, briefings, notifications, email previews). `` wraps Streamdown with code highlighting, math, mermaid, and CJK plugins. - **Server-side: use `convertToModelMessages()` (async) + `toUIMessageStreamResponse()`** — not `toDataStreamResponse()`. Client-side: use `DefaultChatTransport` with `useChat`, not the v5 `api` parameter. - Use `inputSchema` (not `parameters`) and `output`/`outputSchema` (not `result`) for tool definitions — aligned with MCP spec. - Always stream for user-facing AI: use `streamText` + `useChat`, not `generateText`. - `generateObject` and `streamObject` are removed in v6 — use `generateText` / `streamText` with `Output.object()` instead. - **`maxSteps` was removed** — use `stopWhen: stepCountIs(N)` (import `stepCountIs` from `ai`) for multi-step tool calling in both `streamText` and the `Agent` class. - Use the `Agent` class for multi-step reasoning instead of manual tool-calling loops. Agent methods are `agent.generate()` and `agent.stream()` (not `agent.generateText()` / `agent.streamText()`). - Use `DurableAgent` from `@workflow/ai/agent` for production agents that must survive crashes. - **Image generation is gateway-native** — use `model: 'google/gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview'` with `generateText()` for best results (images in `result.files`). Use `experimental_generateImage` only for image-only models (Imagen 4.0, Flux 2). Do NOT use DALL-E or older Gemini 2.x image models — they are outdated. - **Outdated models**: `gpt-4o` → use `gpt-5.4`; `gemini-2.0-flash-exp-image-generation` → use `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview`; DALL-E 2/3 → use Gemini 3.1 Flash Image Preview. - Use `@ai-sdk/mcp` (stable, not experimental) for MCP server connections. - Use `mcp-to-ai-sdk` CLI to generate static tool definitions from MCP servers for security. - Use AI SDK DevTools (`npx @ai-sdk/devtools`) during development for debugging. ### Vercel Platform - Never hardcode secrets — use environment variables via `vercel env` or Marketplace auto-provisioning. - Add `.env*.local` to `.gitignore` — these files contain pulled secrets. - Use Fluid Compute for long-running functions — extends max duration to 800s on paid plans. - Use `waitUntil` (or `after` in Next.js) for background work after sending a response. - Configure cron jobs in `vercel.json` and verify with `CRON_SECRET` header. - Use `vercel deploy --prebuilt` in CI for fastest deploys (separate build from deploy). - For monorepos, use Turborepo with remote caching and `--affected` for efficient CI. --- ## Plugin Mechanics This document is part of the **Vercel plugin for Claude Code**. The plugin uses Claude Code's hook system to automatically inject relevant context as you work. ### SessionStart — Baseline injection On every session event (`startup`, `resume`, `clear`, `compact`), the `inject-claude-md.mjs` hook runs and feeds the entire contents of `vercel.md` (this file) into the conversation as foundational context. This ensures every session starts with the full Vercel ecosystem knowledge graph. **Hook config** (`hooks/hooks.json`): ```json "SessionStart": [{ "matcher": "startup|resume|clear|compact", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node \"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/inject-claude-md.mjs\"" }] }] ``` ### PreToolUse — Skill-map matching Whenever Claude invokes `Read`, `Edit`, `Write`, or `Bash`, the `pretooluse-skill-inject.mjs` hook fires. It matches the tool's target (file path or bash command) against patterns parsed from each skill's SKILL.md frontmatter (`metadata.filePattern` / `metadata.bashPattern`) and injects the corresponding `skills//SKILL.md` files as `additionalContext`. **Matching logic:** - **File tools** (`Read|Edit|Write`): the `file_path` is tested against each skill's `pathPatterns` (glob syntax: `*`, `**`, `?`). Matching tries the full path first, then the basename, then progressively longer suffixes. - **Bash tool**: the `command` string is tested against each skill's `bashPatterns` (regex syntax). - Matched skills are sorted by **priority descending** (higher number = injected first). ### MAX_SKILLS cap and deduplication - At most **3 skills** are injected per hook invocation (`MAX_SKILLS = 3`), subject to an **18KB byte budget**. When more than 3 match, the highest-priority skills win; lower-priority matches are dropped. - **Session dedup**: each skill is injected only once per session. The hook persists injected skill names to a temp file keyed by `session_id` (SHA-256 hashed for long IDs). Subsequent invocations that match the same skill silently skip it. - Set `VERCEL_PLUGIN_HOOK_DEDUP=off` to disable dedup (every match re-injects). Set `RESET_DEDUP=1` to clear the dedup file at the start of an invocation. ### Debug logging Set `VERCEL_PLUGIN_DEBUG=1` or `VERCEL_PLUGIN_HOOK_DEBUG=1` to emit structured JSON-lines to stderr. Debug events include: - `input-parsed` — tool name and session ID received - `skillmap-loaded` — number of skills in the map - `matches-found` — which skills matched and why (pattern + match type) - `dedup-filtered` — skills after dedup filtering - `cap-applied` — emitted when matches exceed MAX_SKILLS, shows selected vs. dropped skills with priorities - `skills-injected` — final list of injected skills - `issue` — structured error with code, message, and hint (e.g., `SKILL_FILE_MISSING`, `BASH_REGEX_INVALID`) - `complete` — result summary with elapsed time and per-phase timing breakdowns

The code checks if `source` is a string, and since it's an object, it incorrectly treats it as a remote plugin instead of handling it as a local source specification. The fix needs to recognize when `source` is an object with `{ source: "local", path: "..." }` and extract the path to process it as a local plugin discovery. I'm checking what test coverage exists for the plugin discovery flow, particularly around how marketplace sources are handled in different formats.

I'm noticing there's a gap in test coverage—no plugin tests exist, and only a single e2e test covers the `install` command, so this regression slipped through undetected. Let me check what the `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS` configuration looked like before this change to understand what broke.

The real issue is becoming clearer: the `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` file uses a codex-style format with nested source objects, but `discoverFromMarketplace` treats anything that isn't a string as a remote source, so local plugins never get discovered. That's the actual bug I need to fix.

To make sure I understand: you're suggesting we drop `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` from discovery (since it's mainly a Codex install target format, not a discovery target) and rely on vendor-specific marketplace files (e.g. `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`) plus `isPluginDir` fallback for codex-only plugins?

Let me double-check what happens for a plugin that only has `.codex-plugin/` and `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` (e.g. one scaffolded by `plugins init --agent codex`):

 The user is clarifying that `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` serves as a universal manifest format, but actual plugin installations should always target the specific agent's vendor directory rather than being installed universally—so we can safely ignore universal installation and focus on agent-specific discovery and installation paths. I'm realizing the core tension: if we remove `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` from discovery entirely, then plugins scaffolded with just `--agent codex` won't be discoverable since they'd only have `.codex-plugin/plugin.json` and the universal marketplace file. So I need to keep `.agents/plugins` in the discovery locations, but maybe reorder it so vendor-specific manifests take precedence when they exist.

Got it. So the cleanest fix is to drop `.agents/plugins` from `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS` — that way:
- `entireio/skills` discovery falls through to `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` (string-source format), which works
- `plugins init`-scaffolded plugins fall through to `isPluginDir()` which detects `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` / `.codex-plugin/plugin.json` and treats root as a single plugin

But you also asked about test coverage. Let me grill on that:

 When `plugins init` runs, it generates vendor-specific plugin manifests for each selected agent, plus a marketplace file in the `.agents/plugins/` directory—but notably, it doesn't create a separate marketplace.json for Claude, since the plugin.json file itself serves as the marketplace entry.

For a freshly scaffolded plugin, discovery finds that `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` file and immediately returns its result, even though the marketplace data contains an invalid source object rather than actual plugins. This causes the `plugins add` command to fail because it sees zero discovered plugins, revealing two underlying issues with how the discovery and marketplace handling work.

The cleanest fix is to remove `.agents/plugins/` from the discovery locations entirely. This way, entireio/skills falls back to its `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` with a valid string source, and init-scaffolded plugins skip marketplace discovery altogether and get detected as plugins through the `isPluginDir()` check. Though I'm considering whether there's value in keeping that location for codex-only scenarios, the simpler approach of just dropping it seems to solve both problems elegantly.

Since they specifically asked about test coverage, I should clarify what level of testing they want — whether a unit test for `discoverPlugins` with the entireio fixture would suffice, or if they're expecting a full e2e test that runs the `plugins add` command.

Same unit tests PLUS an e2e test that spawns `ai-pkgs plugins add ./<local-fixture>` and asserts a `plugin entry added to ai-package.json` outcome — stronger but slower (no Git fetch; uses `--registry file`)
All of the above PLUS unit tests for `plugins targets add/remove/list`, `plugins remove --uninstall`, `plugins update` reinstall — maximum coverage, larger PR

input.overview: Fix the `plugins add` regression where `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` (codex-format with object source) shortcircuits discovery and produces "No plugins found in source". Drop `.agents/plugins` from the marketplace lookup priority and add unit tests for `discoverPlugins`.
input.plan:
# Fix plugins add discovery regression ## Root cause [apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts](apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts) lines 32-38 puts `.agents/plugins` first in `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS`. The file at `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` uses the Codex-style object source: ```json { "plugins": [{ "name": "entire", "source": { "source": "local", "path": "./" } }] } ``` But `discoverFromMarketplace` (line 337) treats any non-string source as a remote plugin and skips it: ```337:344:apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts if (typeof entry.source !== 'string') { remotePlugins.push({ name: entry.name, description: entry.description || undefined, source: entry.source, }); continue; } ``` Result: `plugins` returns empty, `plugins add` prints "No plugins found in source" and exits 1. ## Fix (per user direction) `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` is meaningful as a Codex install target (mirrored to `~/.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` during install), not as a primary discovery source. We install to specific agents, never to `.agents` as a universal target. Drop it from discovery priority and let the existing fallbacks take over. ## Changes ### 1. [apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts](apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts) Remove `.agents/plugins` from `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS`: ```ts const MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS = [ '', '.claude-plugin', '.cursor-plugin', '.codex-plugin', ] as const; ``` Keep `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` in `isPluginDir` checks (line 92) so a codex-only repo with no vendor dir still gets detected as a plugin and routed through `inspectPlugin`. Update the file/function JSDoc to mention that `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` is a publishing artifact, not a discovery target. ## Discovery flow after fix ```mermaid flowchart TD start([discoverPlugins rootDir]) --> mpCheck{any marketplace.json
at root, .claude-plugin,
.cursor-plugin, .codex-plugin?} mpCheck -- yes --> dfm[discoverFromMarketplace
string sources only] mpCheck -- no --> rootCheck{isPluginDir rootDir?} rootCheck -- yes --> single[inspectPlugin rootDir] rootCheck -- no --> scan[scanForPlugins depth=2] ``` Cases: - `entireio/skills` (has `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` w/ string source `./`) -> takes the `dfm` branch and succeeds. - `plugins init`-scaffolded plugin (has `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` only, no marketplace.json) -> falls to `isPluginDir` + `inspectPlugin`. - Codex-only repo (has `.codex-plugin/plugin.json` or `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` only) -> `isPluginDir` returns true via the `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` hint, then `inspectPlugin` reads vendor manifest from `.codex-plugin/` if present, else falls back to `dirName(rootDir)` for name. ### 2. Unit tests (new file) Create [apps/cli/tests/unit/plugins-discover.test.ts](apps/cli/tests/unit/plugins-discover.test.ts) with three scenarios. Each builds a temp fixture using `tmpdir` + `mkdtemp` + `mkdir` + `writeFile`, runs `discoverPlugins`, and removes the dir after. - **entireio-shape**: has `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` (codex object-source format) AND `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` (string source `"./"`), plus `skills/explain/SKILL.md`. Expect `plugins.length === 1`, `plugins[0].name === 'entire'`, `plugins[0].skills.length === 1`. - **init-scaffold-shape**: has `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` (name=`my-plugin`), `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` (no `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`), plus a `skills/example/SKILL.md`. Expect `plugins.length === 1`, `plugins[0].name === 'my-plugin'`. - **codex-only-shape**: has `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` only (no `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`), plus `skills/foo/SKILL.md`. Expect `plugins.length === 1` (via `isPluginDir` hint + `inspectPlugin` returning name = dir basename). Also add an explicit regression assertion: after the fix, a repo with ONLY `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` and `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` (entireio shape) must yield a non-empty `plugins` array. ### 3. Manual smoke test After unit tests pass, manually run `ai-pkgs plugins add entireio/skills` and confirm a plugin is added. ## Validation `bun run type-check:cli && bun run --cwd apps/cli test:unit && bun run --cwd apps/cli test:e2e && bunx biome check apps/cli/src/ apps/cli/tests/`

content.text:
All done. Here's the summary:

**Root cause:** `.agents/plugins` was first in `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS`, so `discoverFromMarketplace` consumed its `marketplace.json` which uses the Codex object-source format (`{ source: 'local', path: './' }`). That format hit the `typeof entry.source !== 'string'` branch and got classified as a remote plugin, leaving the local `plugins` array empty.

**Fix:** Removed `.agents/plugins` from `MARKETPLACE_LOCATIONS` in `apps/cli/src/plugins/discover.ts`. It's a publishing artifact for the Codex installer, not a discovery target. Discovery now falls through to `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` (which uses string sources) or `isPluginDir` fallback. The `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` check in `isPluginDir` remains so codex-only repos still get detected.

**Tests added:** 7 new tests in `apps/cli/tests/unit/plugins-discover.test.ts`:
- 3 `discoverPlugins` scenarios (entireio shape, init-scaffold shape, codex-only shape)
- 4 `isPluginDir` scenarios (agents/plugins, claude-plugin, skills dir, empty dir)

**Validation:** type-check pass, 118 unit tests pass (14 files), 26 e2e tests pass, biome clean, and manual `plugins add entireio/skills` succeeds with the fix.

Good catch. Let me trace the logic to understand the bug before asking questions.

Found it. Two separate problems:

1. **Line 210**: The "Enabled in .claude/settings.json" message fires for both `claude-code` AND `cursor`, unconditionally. But when you install only to `cursor`, you shouldn't see a Claude settings message.

2. **More importantly**, let me check whether the Cursor installer *actually* writes to `.claude/settings.json` on non-Windows:

So on macOS (non-Windows), `installToCursor` calls `installToPluginCache` which **does** write to `~/.claude/settings.json` (the global one, since no `projectDir` is passed). That means when you select only Cursor, the installer:
1. Copies files to `~/.claude/plugins/cache/`
2. Writes to `~/.claude/plugins/installed_plugins.json`
3. Writes `enabledPlugins` into `~/.claude/settings.json`

So it's not just a message bug — the installer actually mutates Claude's settings even when you only asked for Cursor. This is because Cursor shares the Claude plugin cache on non-Windows, which is a reasonable implementation detail, but the user-facing message is misleading.

This is architecturally correct (Cursor reads Claude's plugin cache on non-Windows). But the message 'Enabled in .claude/settings.json' when you only selected Cursor is confusing.

How should we handle this?

content.text:
It seems like you'd prefer me to just lay out my thinking on this rather than go through one-at-a-time Q&A. Let me share my analysis of your proposal and the open questions I see, so you can react to the whole picture at once.

---

**What I understand from your proposal:**

You want `ai-package.json` to have a `workspace` section that maps a skill name to:
- `file`: the local path where you're editing it (e.g. `.cursor/skills/my-skill`)
- `git`: the upstream repo (e.g. `git@github.com:owner/repo`)
- `path`: the subdirectory inside that repo (e.g. `skills/my-skill`)

Then some CLI command (like `ai-pkgs workspace push` or `ai-pkgs workspace sync`) would take your local edits and push them to the upstream repo at the right path.

**The questions I think are important:**

1. **What's the actual action?** When you say "每次迭代都可以发自动帮忙发和更新对应的 SKILL", does the CLI: (a) copy your local files into a clone of the git repo, commit and push? (b) open a PR? (c) just sync files without committing?

2. **Install-to-iterate transition**: You asked "如果我安装了然后想来迭代，应该怎么使用". The installed skill lives at e.g. `.cursor/skills/explain/`. If you add a workspace entry pointing at that same path, the CLI could detect it's already installed and just wire up the git remote. But this only works if you have push access to the upstream repo.

3. **Directory granularity**: You said "文件夹维度" — so the unit is a skill directory, not individual files. That makes sense since skills are already directory-scoped.

4. **Plugin iteration**: You asked about this but said "暂时不管". The natural extension would be `workspace.plugins` with the same shape, but plugins have vendor dirs and marketplace manifests that would need to be synced too — much more complex. Deferring this is smart.

5. **Relationship to `skills` section**: If a skill is in `workspace.skills`, should it also appear in `skills`? Or does `workspace` replace it? My instinct: they're independent — `skills` tracks what you've installed, `workspace` tracks what you're developing. A skill can be in both (installed + being iterated on).

**My honest take:** This is a meaningful feature but has a lot of design surface. Before writing a plan, I'd want to know: are you trying to solve a problem you're hitting *right now* (e.g. you're editing `entireio/skills` locally and manually git-pushing), or is this more of a "nice to have" you want to design for later? That would change whether I'd recommend implementing it now vs. just documenting the design in a new `ai-package-workspace.md`.

What's your thinking?

The user wants to design a feature where users can iterate on skills locally and push updates back to a git repo. Let me think about this more carefully.

The core workflow is:
1. User has a skill (either authored from scratch or forked from an installed one)
2. They edit it locally
3. They want to push changes to a git repo (specific branch or main)

Let me think about the design options and what makes the most sense.

Key questions:
- Is `workspace` the right field name?
- What's the minimal schema?
- What CLI commands are needed?


- How does it interact with the existing `skills` section?

I'm considering two main approaches: either adding a separate `workspace` section that tracks local skills with their git remotes and paths, or extending the existing `skills` entries with metadata flags to indicate they're in development mode. The first approach keeps local development concerns isolated, while the second keeps everything in one place but might clutter the existing structure.

Actually, stepping back—the core use case here is really about a publish and sync workflow: I want to develop a skill locally, test it, and push it to a git repository, or conversely, I might fork an existing skill and iterate on my own version. Let me think through what the user actually needs from a workflow perspective.

The essential information is where the skill lives locally, where it should be pushed remotely, and which branch to target. I'm reconsidering whether "workspace" is the right terminology—in the npm world it refers to monorepo packages, but here it's more about development or publishing configuration. Still, it works if I define it clearly.

The user's request in Chinese suggests they want automatic syncing on each iteration—edit locally, run a command, and the changes push to git. This is essentially git operations with some file management. The simplest approach would be to clone the target repo locally and handle the sync from there.

Looking at the proposed schema, it captures the key mappings well: local file path, git remote, and destination path within the repo. I'm thinking about whether some of these fields could be inferred—the local file location might be determinable from where the skill is installed, and the git details could potentially come from the skill's original source if it was installed from git. So maybe I only need explicit configuration when those defaults don't apply.

Let me check the existing `SkillEntry` type to see what information's already available. It has the source repo, the path within that repo, and the ref, so if I know where the skill lives locally, I can probably derive most of what I need from that existing metadata.

But there's a separate case here—what if someone wants to work on a brand new skill that isn't in the manifest yet? That's a different workflow. I'm thinking the cleanest approach would be a `skills workspace` subcommand that lets you link local skills, push changes, and pull updates, with the workspace config stored in `ai-package.json`. Though I should actually focus on what the user asked—whether their proposed `workspace` field shape makes sense for what they're trying to do.

Looking at their structure, I'm noticing some ambiguities. The `file` path could resolve to different locations depending on the environment, so maybe it should just be a relative path from the project root. The `git` field raises a question about format—should it accept a full URL or a shorthand like `owner/repo`, and should it match the same format as the `skills add` command? The `path` field within the repo makes sense and aligns with existing patterns. But I don't see where the branch gets specified, which the user mentioned matters.

Actually, I'm wondering if I'm overcomplicating this. If the manifest already tracks skills with their source, path, and ref, then for local iteration I really just need to know where the local copy lives. That could be discovered automatically from agent targets, so maybe the workspace only needs to track skills that either don't have a manifest entry or have a different local path than expected.

Let me simplify this. I could add a `workspace` key to `ai-package.json` that just tracks the local skill locations...

Defining CLI commands to manage workspace entries...

And the push workflow would handle cloning the remote, copying changes, and pushing them back.

OK so when a user installs a skill from git, we already store `source` (e.g. `entireio/skills`), `path` (e.g. `skills/explain`), `ref` (e.g. `main`), and `commitSha`. That's most of the remote info we need.

The key missing piece is: **where is the local editable copy?** — that depends on which agent target it was installed to.

Let me check how agent targets resolve skill directories.

Good. Now I have the full picture. Let me think about this carefully.

The key insight: when a skill is installed from git via `skills add`, the manifest already stores `source`, `packageId`, `path`, `ref`, and `commitSha`. The installed skill ends up in e.g. `.cursor/skills/my-skill/`. So for an **already-installed** skill, we have everything we need — both the local location and the remote origin.

The `workspace` field is only needed for skills that **aren't yet in the manifest** or where the local path differs from the agent install path.

But actually — there's a simpler question first:

questions.prompt:
I've looked at the existing manifest structure. An installed skill already stores `source` (e.g. `entireio/skills`), `path` (e.g. `skills/explain`), `ref` (e.g. `main`), and `commitSha` in `ai-package.json`. The installed copy lives at e.g. `.cursor/skills/explain/`.

So for the common case of 'I installed a skill, now I want to iterate and push changes back', we don't need a `workspace` field at all — the existing `skills` entry already has the git remote info, and we can discover the local path from agent targets.

The `workspace` field would only be needed for a new skill being authored from scratch that hasn't been installed yet.

Given this, what's the right approach?

options.label: No workspace field. Add a `skills dev push <name> [--branch <branch>]` command that reads the existing manifest entry to find the remote, and discovers the local installed path automatically. For NEW skills, use `skills dev init <name> --git owner/repo --path skills/my-skill` to create the manifest entry first. (Recommended -- minimal schema change, reuses existing data)
Keep the workspace field for both cases. It's explicit: workspace entries are things you're actively developing, manifest entries are things you've installed. Separation of concerns.
Add a `dev: true` flag to existing SkillEntry. When `dev` is set, the CLI knows this skill is being iterated on and shows it in `skills dev list`. No new top-level field needed.

questions.prompt:
I actually agree a separate field makes sense. Here's why: the `skills` array represents **dependencies** (things you consume). A workspace entry represents **authorship** (things you produce/edit). Mixing them muddies the intent.

But I'd challenge the name `workspace` and the nested structure. Here's my counter-proposal:

```json
{
  "skills": [ ... ],
  "plugins": [ ... ],
  "dev": {
    "my-skill": {
      "local": ".cursor/skills/my-skill",
      "remote": "owner/repo",
      "path": "skills/my-skill",
      "branch": "main"
    }
  }
}
```

`dev` instead of `workspace` because:
- `workspace` in JS ecosystem means monorepo packages (npm/yarn/pnpm workspaces)
- `dev` clearly says 'these are things I'm developing'
- Flat key (skill name -> config) instead of nested `workspace.skills` since we're only doing skills for now

The workflow:
- `ai-pkgs dev add <name>` -- set up a dev entry (interactive: asks local path, remote, branch)
- `ai-pkgs dev push <name> [--branch feat/x]` -- copy local -> clone, commit, push
- `ai-pkgs dev pull <name>` -- pull latest from remote into local path
- `ai-pkgs dev list` -- show all dev entries

For the 'installed skill I want to iterate on' case:
- `ai-pkgs dev add explain` detects it's already in `skills`, pre-fills `remote`/`path`/`branch` from the manifest entry, asks for `local` path (defaults to the installed location).

Does `dev` as the field name + flat structure work for you, or do you prefer `workspace` with the nested `skills` key?

options.label: Use `dev` with flat structure (recommended)
Keep `workspace.skills` nested structure (future-proofs for workspace.plugins)
Use `dev.skills` nested structure (best of both: clear name + future-proof)

content.text: OK so you want `workspace.skills` as the field, and the interesting use case is: **I installed from source A, but I might want to push my iteration to source B (a different repo, like my fork)**. That's a real fork-and-publish workflow.

options.label: Yes, this split makes sense -- origin in `skills`, push target in `workspace`
Too complex -- workspace.skills should be self-contained, don't rely on the skills array at all
Not quite -- let me explain what I mean

content.text: Clean. Now I know exactly what you want. Let me nail down the remaining design questions.

questions.prompt:
Schema is clear. Now the CLI commands. Here's my proposal:

**`ai-pkgs dev link <name>`** -- Create a workspace entry. Interactive: asks for local path, source (git URL), remote path, branch. If the skill is already in the `skills` array, pre-fills source/path/branch from the manifest.

**`ai-pkgs dev push <name>`** -- Clone/fetch the remote, copy `local` dir -> clone at `path`, commit with auto-message, push to the locked branch. Updates `version` in workspace entry with new commitSha.

**`ai-pkgs dev pull <name>`** -- Fetch latest from remote at `path`, copy into `local`. Updates `version`.

**`ai-pkgs dev status [name]`** -- Compare local vs last-pushed version. Show dirty/clean.

**`ai-pkgs dev unlink <name>`** -- Remove workspace entry (doesn't delete local files).

**`ai-pkgs dev list`** -- Show all workspace entries.

Two questions:
1. Is `dev` the right command group name? Or `workspace`?
2. Is `link` the right verb for creating the entry? Or `add`/`init`/`track`?

options.label: Use `dev link/push/pull/status/unlink/list` (recommended -- `dev` is short, `link` implies connecting local to remote)
Use `workspace link/push/pull/status/unlink/list` (matches the field name)
Use `dev add/push/pull/status/remove/list` (matches skills add/remove naming)

questions.prompt:
OK, `workspace` (alias `ws`) as the command group. What verb for creating a workspace entry?

`ai-pkgs workspace link explain` -- connects local skill to a remote
`ai-pkgs workspace unlink explain` -- disconnects

Or:

`ai-pkgs workspace add explain` / `workspace remove explain`

My recommendation: `link/unlink` because it communicates the bidirectional connection better than add/remove (which sounds like install/uninstall). And it avoids confusion with `skills add`.

options.label: link / unlink (recommended)
add / remove (consistent with skills)
track / untrack (git-style)

questions.prompt:
`skills add owner/repo --workspace` is a nice shortcut. It would:

1. Run normal `skills add` (resolve, discover, install, write manifest)
2. Then also create a `workspace.skills` entry with `local` = the installed path, `source` = the git source, `path` = the skill path in repo, `version` = `ref@commitSha`

So one command gives you an installed skill that's immediately ready for iteration. I like it.

One clarification: when using `--workspace`, the skill gets BOTH a `skills[]` manifest entry AND a `workspace.skills` entry. The `skills[]` entry tracks the upstream dependency. The `workspace.skills` entry is for your dev loop. They can diverge (e.g. you push to a fork via workspace while skills[] still points to upstream).

Does that sound right?

options.label: Yes -- both entries, they can diverge
No -- --workspace should ONLY create the workspace entry, skip the skills[] entry
No -- workspace entry should replace the skills[] entry (move it, not duplicate)

questions.prompt:
Got it. `--workspace` means workspace-only, no `skills[]` entry.

Now the push mechanics. When `ai-pkgs workspace push explain` runs:

1. Read workspace entry: `{ local: '.cursor/skills/explain', source: 'github:entireio/skills', path: 'skills/explain', version: 'main@abc123' }`
2. Clone/fetch `entireio/skills` into a temp dir (reuse git cache)
3. Copy contents of `.cursor/skills/explain/` -> `<clone>/skills/explain/` (overwrite)
4. `git add . && git commit -m 'Update explain skill'`
5. `git push origin main`
6. Update workspace entry `version` to `main@<new-commit-sha>`

Question: The commit message. Should it be:
A) Auto-generated: `'chore: update <skill-name>'`
B) Prompted: ask the user for a commit message (with a default)
C) Flag-based: `--message 'my message'` with auto-default

options.label: Prompt for commit message with a sensible default (TTY), auto-generate in --ai mode (recommended)
Always use --message flag, auto-generate if omitted
Always auto-generate, don't ask

content.text:
Good question. Let me think about this from first principles.

There are three states a skill can be in:

1. **Dependency** (`skills[]` entry): "I use this skill, I got it from upstream, I don't modify it." Normal `skills add`.

2. **Workspace** (`workspace.skills` entry): "I'm actively developing this skill, I own it, I push changes to a remote." `workspace link` or `skills add --workspace`.

3. **Both**: "I installed this skill AND I'm developing it." This is the fork scenario — you want to track upstream AND push your changes.

The question is: should workspace and dependency be exclusive or can they coexist?

questions.prompt:
Let me lay out the three real-world scenarios:

**Scenario A -- Author from scratch:**
I create a skill locally, I want to publish it to my repo.
-> Only needs a `workspace.skills` entry. No `skills[]` needed.
-> `workspace link` or `skills add --workspace` with a local path.

**Scenario B -- Pure consumer:**
I install someone else's skill, I never modify it.
-> Only needs a `skills[]` entry. No workspace needed.
-> `skills add owner/repo`

**Scenario C -- Fork and iterate:**
I install a skill from `entireio/skills`, I want to modify it and push changes to `my-fork/skills`.
-> Needs `skills[]` (tracks upstream origin) AND `workspace.skills` (tracks my fork as push target)?
-> OR just `workspace.skills` with source pointing to my fork?

I think Scenario C is the interesting one. My recommendation:

- `workspace.skills` entries are **exclusive** with `skills[]`. If a skill is in workspace, it's NOT in skills[].
- Reason: a workspace skill IS installed (via `local` path). Having it in both arrays means two sources of truth for the same skill on disk.
- If you want to track the original upstream, add an `upstream` field to the workspace entry.

So the schema becomes:
```json
"workspace": {
  "skills": {
    "explain": {
      "local": ".cursor/skills/explain",
      "source": "github:my-fork/skills",
      "path": "skills/explain",
      "version": "main@abc123",
      "upstream": "github:entireio/skills"
    }
  }
}
```

`upstream` is optional -- only set when forked from another source.

options.label: Exclusive -- workspace replaces skills[], optional `upstream` field for fork tracking (recommended)
They can coexist -- skills[] = upstream dependency, workspace = push target, both reference the same local files
Exclusive, no upstream tracking -- if you fork, you lose the upstream reference (simpler)

content.text:
Yes, exactly. If a skill named `explain` is in `workspace.skills`, it will NOT also be in the `skills[]` array. One skill lives in one place only:

- `skills[]` = I'm a **consumer** — I installed it, I don't push changes
- `workspace.skills` = I'm a **developer** — I edit it locally, I push changes to a remote

If you start as a consumer (`skills add entireio/skills --skill explain`) and later want to iterate, running `workspace link explain` would **move** the entry from `skills[]` to `workspace.skills`, preserving the source/path/version info and adding the `local` path.

Going the other direction: `workspace unlink explain` would move it back to `skills[]` (freeze the current version as a dependency again).

questions.prompt:
Does this exclusive model with move semantics (link moves skills[] -> workspace, unlink moves workspace -> skills[]) make sense?

And for the fork case: `upstream` field tracks the original source you got it from, `source` field is where YOU push to (could be the same repo or your fork).

options.label: Yes, exclusive with move semantics makes sense
Actually I want them to coexist -- I want to track upstream in skills[] AND have my workspace push target separately
I need to think about this more -- let's move on to other questions first

content.text:
That simplifies things a lot. So:

- **No fork/upstream concept**. `source` always equals where the skill came from AND where you push to. You must have push access.
- If you want to push to a different repo, you create that repo yourself first, then `workspace link` with that repo as the source.
- `skills add entireio/skills --workspace` means: install the skill AND put it in workspace, with `source: github:entireio/skills` — you're saying "I have push access to this repo and I want to iterate on it".

This means we can drop the `upstream` field entirely.

options.label: Prompt for commit message in TTY, auto-generate in --ai mode (recommended)
Always require --message flag, auto-generate if omitted
Always auto-generate, never ask

content.text: Good question. The conflict scenario: you run `workspace push explain`, but someone else has pushed to the same branch since your last pull, so your push gets rejected.

questions.prompt:
Conflict scenario: `git push` fails because the remote branch has new commits you don't have locally.

Options:

**A) Fail and tell the user to pull first:**
`workspace push` fails with: "Remote has newer commits. Run `ai-pkgs workspace pull explain` first, resolve any conflicts manually, then push again."
Simple, safe, no data loss risk. This is how normal git works.

**B) Auto-rebase: pull --rebase then push:**
The CLI does `git pull --rebase` automatically before pushing. If rebase has conflicts, abort and tell the user. If clean, push succeeds.
Convenient but can silently reorder commits.

**C) Force push (dangerous):**
`workspace push --force` overwrites remote. Only with explicit flag.

My recommendation: (A) as default behavior. The skill directory is a simple folder copy, so `workspace pull` would just overwrite local files with remote contents — no merge needed since skills are typically small, self-contained directories. If the user's local changes would be lost by pull, warn them.

Actually, here's the deeper issue: `workspace push` copies your LOCAL folder into a fresh clone, then commits. It's not a traditional git workflow where you have a persistent local repo. So conflicts can only happen if the remote `path` directory changed since your `version` commitSha. In that case:
- Pull first: fetch remote, overwrite local dir, update version
- Then edit again
- Then push

Since we're copying whole directories (not tracking individual file diffs), this is simpler than normal git conflicts.

options.label: A -- Fail with 'pull first' message (recommended, safe, simple)
B -- Auto pull-rebase before push
C -- Support --force to override, otherwise fail

questions.prompt:
If pull + auto-rebase still has conflicts (e.g. both you AND someone else edited the same file in the skill directory), there are only two real options:

**A) Bail out and hand off to the user:**
The CLI tells you: "Merge conflict in `skills/explain/SKILL.md`. The cloned repo is at `/tmp/ai-pkgs-ws-xxxxx/`. Resolve the conflict there, then run `ai-pkgs workspace push explain --from /tmp/ai-pkgs-ws-xxxxx/`".
The user resolves it manually in the temp clone with their editor/git tools, then tells the CLI to finish the push from that resolved state.

**B) Always overwrite (your local wins):**
Since we're copying whole directories, just always overwrite the remote path with your local directory. This means `push` = "replace the remote skill dir with my local copy". No merge at all.
This is actually the simplest mental model: your local IS the source of truth, push just publishes it.
The risk: if someone else added a file you don't have locally, it gets deleted.

My recommendation: (B). Here's why:
- Skills are small, self-contained directories (usually 1-5 files)
- The workspace user IS the author — they own the content
- `push` means "publish my local state" — like `rsync --delete`
- If you want to incorporate someone else's changes first, run `pull` before editing
- This avoids ALL merge complexity

The workflow becomes dead simple:
1. `pull` = remote overwrites local (get latest)
2. Edit locally
3. `push` = local overwrites remote (publish yours)

No merge. No rebase. No conflict resolution. Just copy directions.

options.label: B -- push = local overwrites remote, pull = remote overwrites local (recommended, dead simple)
A -- bail out and let user resolve in temp clone
Try to merge file-by-file, bail only on real conflicts

questions.prompt:
OK, so the full `workspace push` flow is:

1. Clone/fetch the remote repo (reuse git cache)
2. Checkout the locked branch from `version`
3. `git pull --rebase origin <branch>` to get latest
4. Copy local skill dir -> clone at `path` (overwrite the directory)
5. `git add . && git commit -m '<message>'`
6. `git push origin <branch>`
7. If push succeeds: update `version` in ai-package.json to `branch@newSha`
8. If rebase had conflicts: print the temp clone path, tell user to resolve and re-run

And `workspace pull`:
1. Clone/fetch the remote at latest
2. Copy clone's `path` dir -> local dir (overwrite)
3. Update `version` to `branch@latestSha`

I think we've nailed the design. One last question: scope of the first implementation. What do you want built now?

Core only: link, unlink, push, pull, list (skip status and --workspace shortcut for now)
Design doc first (like ai-package-plugins-v2.md), implement later

input.plan:
# Workspace Design Doc

Write `docs/ai-package-workspace.md` — the technical design document for the `workspace` feature that enables skill iteration (local editing + git push/pull).

## Document structure

The document will follow the same format as [docs/ai-package-plugins-v2.md](docs/ai-package-plugins-v2.md): problem statement, manifest shape, command tree, per-command ASCII TUI mockups, field rules, and workflow diagrams.

### 1. Command Tree (first section, per user request)

```
ai-pkgs workspace (alias: ws)
  ├── link <name>           Link a skill for local iteration
  ├── unlink <name>         Move a workspace skill back to skills[]
  ├── push <name>           Push local changes to remote
  ├── pull <name>           Pull latest from remote into local
  ├── status [name]         Show dirty/clean state vs last push
  └── list                  List all workspace skills

ai-pkgs skills add <source> --workspace
  └── Install directly into workspace.skills (skip skills[])
```

### 2. Manifest shape

```json
{
  "skills": { ... },
  "plugins": { ... },
  "workspace": {
    "skills": {
      "explain": {
        "local": ".cursor/skills/explain",
        "source": "github:entireio/skills",
        "path": "skills/explain",
        "version": "main@c376dc9"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Field definitions:
- `local` — relative path from project root to the skill directory on disk
- `source` — git remote in the same format as `skills[].source` (e.g. `github:owner/repo`, `git@host:owner/repo.git`)
- `path` — directory inside the remote repo containing this skill
- `version` — `branch@commitSha` — the branch is locked (set at link time, cannot be changed), commitSha updates on push/pull

### 3. Key design rules to document

- **Exclusive**: a skill is in `skills[]` OR `workspace.skills`, never both
- **`workspace link`** moves an entry from `skills[]` to `workspace.skills`; asks for `local` path (defaults to installed agent path)
- **`workspace unlink`** moves back to `skills[]` (freezes current version)
- **`skills add --workspace`** installs directly into `workspace.skills`, never touches `skills[]`
- **`skills add/update/outdated`** ignores workspace skills
- **`workspace push`** flow: clone/fetch remote -> checkout branch -> pull --rebase -> copy local dir to clone path -> commit -> push -> update version. On conflict: bail, print temp clone path, user resolves manually
- **`workspace pull`** flow: clone/fetch remote -> copy clone path to local dir -> update version
- **Commit message**: TTY prompts with default `"chore: update <skill-name>"`; `--message` flag; auto-generate in `--ai` mode
- **No fork/upstream tracking**: source = where you push. If you want to push to a different repo, create it yourself and use that as source

### 4. ASCII TUI mockups to include

Every command gets a full TTY mockup showing clack-style prompts, spinners, notes, and success/error messages. Also include `--ai` mode output for each. At minimum:

- `workspace link` (from existing skill, from scratch)
- `workspace unlink`
- `workspace push` (clean push, conflict bail-out)
- `workspace pull`
- `workspace status` (clean, dirty)
- `workspace list` (with entries, empty)
- `skills add owner/repo --workspace`

### 5. Error cases to document

- `workspace push` when remote has diverged (conflict)
- `workspace link` on a skill that's already in workspace
- `workspace link` on a skill name that doesn't exist in `skills[]` and no `--source` provided
- `workspace push` without push access to remote
- `workspace pull` would overwrite local changes (warn first)

## File to create

- [docs/ai-package-workspace.md](docs/ai-package-workspace.md)
